facts.fact: The first US presidential election was held in 1789, and George Washington ran unopposed.
Wyoming was the first state to grant women the right to vote in 1869 — 51 years before the 19th Amendment.
The Electoral College has 538 electors — equal to 435 House members + 100 Senators + 3 for Washington D.C.
Five presidents have won the presidency while losing the popular vote: J.Q. Adams, Hayes, Harrison, Bush (2000), and Trump (2016).
The 26th Amendment lowered the voting age from 21 to 18 in 1971, largely driven by the Vietnam War era.
In the 2020 election, over 159 million Americans voted — the highest turnout rate since 1900.
Maine and Nebraska are the only states that don't use a winner-take-all system for electoral votes.
The longest presidential inauguration speech was William Henry Harrison's at 8,445 words — he died 31 days later.
Early voting has grown dramatically — in 2020, over 101 million people voted before Election Day.
The US is one of the few democracies where voter registration is not automatic — citizens must actively register.
The 'I Voted' sticker tradition began in the 1980s as a way to encourage voter turnout through social pressure.
Super Tuesday got its name in the 1980s — it's when the most states hold primaries on a single day.
The presidential debates weren't a regular feature until 1976 — the first televised one was Kennedy vs. Nixon in 1960.
The 20th Amendment moved Inauguration Day from March 4 to January 20, starting in 1937.
Ranked-choice voting is used in Alaska, Maine, and several cities — voters rank candidates by preference.
